coredata.dc:title: Pivotal role of Boron-doped graphene quantum dots in stretchable and Self-Healable red emission nanocomposite Film: One step advance for white light emitting diodes application
coredata.dc:creator: Hsu, Chen-Chu
Laysandra, Livy
Chiu, Yu-Cheng
Liu, Wei-Ren
coredata.dc:description: In this study, red-emitting BGQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method derived from 1,3,6-Trinitropyrene (TNP) and boric acid. The luminescence properties of as-synthesized BGQDs were characterized through photoluminescence (PL), time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL), and UV–Vis measurements. After the modification of boron doping, B-GQDs demonstrated superior optical properties and chemical stability compared to pristine ones. For the sake of continued stability in the luminescent layer performance derived from BGQDs, poly{(n-butyl acrylate)–co-[N-(hydroxymethyl)acrylamide]} (PBA0.8-co-PNMA0.2) as an amphiphilic copolymer matrix with tunable mechanical, elastic, and self-healing properties was introduced. Through the synergistic effects between BGQDs and amphiphilic copolymer side chain, the physical and mechanical properties of the red-emitting nanocomposite film were impressively improved. Furthermore, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) were successfully fabricated by combining a 450 nm GaN chip, a commercial green-emitting (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ phosphor, and a self-healable red-emitting nanocomposite film, generating a high color rendering index (CRI) value of 89.4.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the x% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film
Mutually synergistic effect of BGQDs/AP to the physical, mechanical, self-healing, and self-recoverability properties of nanocomposites
x% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film for LED devices

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledged to National Science of Technology Council (NSTC) project grant no. NSTC 111-2622-E-033-007, 111-2811-E-033-001-MY3, 111-2221-E-033 -004 -MY3, 111-2923-E-006-009 and 111-3116-F-011-005. We acknowledge Professor Wei-Hung Chiang and Darwin Kurniawan for the technical assistance of the MicroRaman (JASCO 5100 spectrometer) and Photoluminescence (HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer) measurements. Additional data including PL, UV, Raman, FFT, XPS, and SEM for detailed characterization of GQDs and/or BGQDs as well as H NMR, GPC, FTIR, TGA, and DSC for detailed characterization of polymer and/or nanocomposite. This material is available free of charge via the Internet.

The authors gratefully acknowledged to National Science of Technology Council (NSTC) project grant no. We acknowledge Professor Wei-Hung Chiang and Darwin Kurniawan for the technical assistance of the MicroRaman (JASCO 5100 spectrometer) and Photoluminescence (HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer) measurements.

head.ce:title: Pivotal role of Boron-doped graphene quantum dots in stretchable and Self-Healable red emission nanocomposite Film: One step advance for white light emitting diodes application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, red-emitting BGQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method derived from 1,3,6-Trinitropyrene (TNP) and boric acid. The luminescence properties of as-synthesized BGQDs were characterized through photoluminescence (PL), time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL), and UV–Vis measurements. After the modification of boron doping, B-GQDs demonstrated superior optical properties and chemical stability compared to pristine ones. For the sake of continued stability in the luminescent layer performance derived from BGQDs, poly{(n-butyl acrylate)–co-[N-(hydroxymethyl)acrylamide]} (PBA0.8-co-PNMA0.2) as an amphiphilic copolymer matrix with tunable mechanical, elastic, and self-healing properties was introduced. Through the synergistic effects between BGQDs and amphiphilic copolymer side chain, the physical and mechanical properties of the red-emitting nanocomposite film were impressively improved. Furthermore, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) were successfully fabricated by combining a 450 nm GaN chip, a commercial green-emitting (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ phosphor, and a self-healable red-emitting nanocomposite film, generating a high color rendering index (CRI) value of 89.4.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) have gained significant popularity as solid-state lighting devices in recent years, owing to their longer lifespan, energy efficiency, and reduced heat dissipation [1–5]. However, the combination of conventional gallium nitride (GaN) semiconductor materials with commercial cerium-doped yellow-emitting phosphors (YAG) is not conducive to achieving a high Color Rendering Index (CRI) value [6]. Consequently, there is a growing interest in research endeavors aimed at exploring alternative high-efficiency luminescence materials to replace YAG.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that have attracted significant attention as a novel optoelectronic material. This interest stems from their distinctive electronic and optical properties, which originate from the quantum confinement effect and edge state luminescence mechanism that are characteristic of GQDs. [7]. Additionally, their remarkable biocompatibility and environmental friendliness make them highly promising for various applications in the fields of biomedicine and environmental science, such as biological imaging [8,9], metal ion sensing [10,11], and even in the optoelectronic industry for applications like light-emitting diodes [12–14] and solar cell technology [15,16]. However, the characteristics of GQDs can vary depending on the synthesis method used. Generally, these synthetic methods can be categorized into two groups: top-down and bottom-up approaches [17–19]. Top-down synthesis methods typically use larger carbon-based precursors, such as graphene, fullerenes, and carbon nanotubes. While this synthetic method is suitable for large-scale production, it is challenging to control the structure and size of the resulting materials such as ultrasonic oscillation and electrochemical oxidation [20,21]. Zhuo et al. carried out the oxidation of graphene by using concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 at room temperature and subsequently processed it using ultrasonic vibration to obtain a blue-emitting solution of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with an emitted wavelength of 407 nm and an absolute quantum yield of 3.4% [22]. On the other hand, the bottom-up method involves the manipulated synthesis or carbonization of organic molecules or polymers such as the hydrothermal method or molecular carbonization method [23,24]. Dong et al. handled the degree of citric acid carbonization to prepare a solution of graphene quantum dots that emit blue light at a wavelength of 460 nm. This solution exhibited emission activity independent of excitation wavelength, and the quantum yield was calculated to be about 9% [25].

Furthermore, numerous studies have indicated that the intrinsic properties of GQDs can be effectively modulated by doping with heteroatoms. Among these, S and N dopants have been reported as the most common to ameliorate optical properties of GQDs. For instance, Li et al. synthesized sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) exhibiting blue-green luminescence through the electrolysis of graphite in a sodium p-toluenesulfonate aqueous solution. S-GQDs showcased higher luminescence intensity and improved electronic and surface chemical properties in comparison to GQDs. Furthermore, the quantum yield of S-GQDs was determined to be 10.6% when excited at 380 nm. [26]. Yang et al. synthesized nitrogen doped GQDs (NGQDs) in the nature of blue luminescence by one-pot hydrothermal method. Under excitation at a wavelength of 360 nm, the as-synthesized N-GQDs quantum yield is 25% and also applied in WLED with the CIE coordinates was (0.357, 0.315) and the color temperature was 4427 K [27].

To be more practical in addressing longer service life, incorporating GQD derivatives into flexible and self-healing polymer matrix types is the most preferred way [28–30]. Thus, GQD derivatives employed in optoelectronic applications are capable of maintaining their function, fault-tolerant, durable, and shape-resistant. Great efforts have been devoted to constructing flexible and self-healing polymers based on different mechanisms such as covalent bonds (i.e., Diels-Alder reaction and disulfide bonds) and noncovalent bonds (i.e., hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, π–π interactions, and host–guest interactions) [31–33]. It was discovered that autonomous self-healing capabilities based on hydrogen bonding networks are the most popular due to their simplicity and versatile non-covalent interactions which have been employed in various fields of application such as in the advancement of “smart” textiles [34–36], electronic skins [37–39], mobile devices [40], self-healing functional surfaces [41–43], and supercapacitors [44]. In previous reports by Chiu's group, poly{(n-butyl acrylate)–co-[N-(hydroxymethyl)acrylamide]} (PBAx-co-PNMAy) has been introduced as a self-cross-linkable and self-healable amphiphilic copolymer, where a good compromise between mechanical properties and self-healing efficiency is successfully achieved through the ease of controlling the monomer ratio and varying the self-crosslinking degree [45,46]. The BA unit acts as a soft segment with hydrophobic nature contributing to maintaining the original mechanics and functionality of the copolymer when exposed to a high-humidity environment. The NMA unit with − OH, −NH, and a lone electron pair on the C = O groups, plays an important role in providing self-healing capabilities based on dynamic hydrogen bonding networks as well as a thermal-triggered self-cross-linker to achieve the desired mechanical strength and flexible features. Thus, the utilization of such amphiphilic copolymer as a matrix for nanomaterials is significant to enrich BGQDs with more functions and expand their range of applications.

In this work, we have applied boron-doped graphene quantum dots for the first time in WLEDs. We prepared GQDs by a hydrothermal reaction with Dimethylformamide and 1,3,6-Trinitropyrene, and then modified the as-prepared GQDs using boric acid to obtain BGQDs, which have uniform sizes, excitation-dependent PL, enhanced luminesce and red-shifting emission. In the interest of continued stability in luminescent layer performance derived from BGQDs nanomaterial for WLEDs application, excellent mechanical strength and toughness, elastic, thermal stability, and self-healable red-emitting nanocomposite film was proposed by incorporating BGQDs into PBA0.8-co-PNMA0.2 matrix via a simple blending method. Merits the abundance of oxygen- and amine-containing groups that are readily available to form H-bond interactions with the NMA segment in the copolymer system, BGQDs also occupy a second important role as external H-bond cross-linkers. The synergistic effect of the optimized hydrogen bonding between BGQDs nanoparticles and copolymer chains was further studied, which resulted in a well-connected dynamic covalent and non-covalent bonds network and dramatically improves material performance that can be tuned on demand. We also provide green emitting film through the mixing process of epoxy resin as a matrix with (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ phosphors as a green emitting source. Finally, all luminescent films are arranged from green to red emission over the surface of the blue light chip for WLED applications.

Boric acid (H3BO3, 99.5%) was purchased from J.T.-Baker. Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%) was purchased from Acros Organics. Propylene Glycol Methyl Ether Acetate (PGMEA, 99%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. N-(hydroxymethyl)acrylamide (NMA) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Japan. Butyl acrylate (BA) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and the inhibitors were removed prior to use by passing through a basic alumina column. 2,2′-Azobis(2-methyl propionitrile) (AIBN) was supplied by UniRegion Bio-Tech (Hsinchu, Taiwan).

BGQDs were synthesized using a conventional hydrothermal method. Specifically, 0.5 g of TNP and 0.01 mol of boric acid were combined with 30 mL of DMF and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixture underwent sonication for an additional 30 min to ensure proper dispersion of TNP and boric acid in the solvent. The resulting solution was then transferred to a 100 mL PTFE autoclave and subjected to a reaction at 180 °C for 24 h. Once cooled, impurities were eliminated through vacuum filtration, resulting in the production of BGQD powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the x% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film

ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of high-quality WLEDs involved the utilization of a commercial green-emitting (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ phosphor and the 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film prepared in this study. Firstly, (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ phosphors with varying weights were uniformly mixed with an epoxy resin base (referred to as A gel) and an epoxy resin curing agent (referred to as B gel) in a 1:1 mass ratio. The resulting mixture was then carefully applied onto a 450 nm GaN chip and dried in a convection oven at 120 °C for 24 h. Lastly, the dried LEDs were coated with the 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) imaging were performed using a JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV, and the magnification range from 50 to 1.5 M.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging was conducted using a CrabiAFM-E-01 instrument, after dissolving the sample in alcohol, drop-casting it onto a silicon substrate, and subsequently drying it in an 80 °C oven for 12 h.

MicroRaman measurement (JASCO 5100 spectrometer) was conducted by scanning the XY plane with the laser beam over an area of 10 × 10 μm2 in a 1 μm step at room temperature with the laser excitation wavelength of 1 ¼ 532 nm and carried out using laser power of 4.0 mW. Raman mapping results are based on the average results of 100 random positions on each sample.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using a Thermo VG-Scientific Sigma Probe spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα excitation source.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired using a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer, while the time-resolved decay lifetimes of the samples were determined using an integrating sphere FluoTime 300. Changes in the sample's absorbance were monitored using a Hitachi U-2900 UV–visible spectrophotometer, and the determination wavelength range was 200–800 cm−1.
1H NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker Avance III HD-600 (600 MHz) at 21 °C in DMSO‑d 6. A peak at 2.5 ppm is referred to as the residual solvent signal.

The number-average molecular weight (Mn) and molecular weight distribution (PDI = Mw/Mn) were measured by a Jasco GPC-900 instrument·THF was used as the eluent with a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min at 25 °C. The calibration of the molecular weights was based on PS standards.

To probe the interactions between flexible and self-healable copolymer matrix with BGQDs, a series of film samples consisting of pristine copolymer and nanocomposites before and after crosslinking reaction were analyzed by Attenuated total reflection - Fourier transform infrared (ATR - FTIR) on Spectrum Two (Perkin Elmer) in the range of 650 – 4,000 cm−1. The thin films were dried overnight under vacuum before measurement.

Thermal degradation analysis of 6 h crosslinked specimens consisting of amphiphilic copolymer and nanocomposite loaded with different concentrations of BGQD was carried out with a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, 550/TA) in the range of 25–700 °C at a constant heating rate of 10 °C min−1 and a nitrogen gas flow rate of 50 mL min−1. Prior to measurement, the specimens were dried at 50 °C under vacuum for 6 h to remove excess water.

The glass transition temperatures of the specimens were observed by Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) analysis and performed on DSC2-00573 (192.168.0.5). Dry specimens of 5 mg were sealed in 40 µL aluminum pans. Then, each specimen was cycled two times from −40 to 110 °C at a heating/cooling rate of 10 °C min−1 under a nitrogen gas flow rate of 50 mL min−1.

The stress–strain curves of the specimens were collected using a Shimadzu - EZ-EX universal testing machine setup, and the data were recorded with Trapezium X software. The extension rate during testing was set to 50 mm/min. Each measurement was conducted at least three times under the same conditions. To gain further insight into the effect of extension rates on self-recoverability thin films, cyclic tensile tests at different extension rates of 50 and 10 mm/min were proposed. The specimens were initially stretched to a maximum strain of 100% and then recovered at the same speed with a resting time of 1 min before proceeding to the next cycle.

Micro-thickness film for each sample was prepared by spin-coating 200 mg/mL sample onto the microscope slide glass substrate with a spinning speed of 1000 rpm for 60 s. Then, proceed with the thermal cross-linking reaction at 90 °C for 6 h. The ready micro-thickness film was scratched using a single-sided blade and leave it at room temperature under short periodic times of 0, 0.5, and 2 h. The preliminary evaluation of the scratch recoverability of each sample was observed via an optical microscope (OM, Olympus Microscope BX53).

The as-prepared rectangular shape samples were sliced into two pieces by a razor blade and then the fracture surface between the two pieces was immediately kept in contact at room temperature for 24 h. To evaluate the self-healing efficiency quantitatively, a comparison of the tensile test results between the control sample (undamaged sample) and the healed sample was performed with an extension rate of 50 mm min−1. The healing percentage is defined as the toughness of the healed sample related to that without damage.

Details on the apparatus and methods used to measure the stability of the red emission under stretching conditions were described previously [48]. The red-emission stability measurement of x% (w/w)@BGQD/AP nanocomposite . films during the loading cyclic tensile test were carried out as follows: after being temporarily suspended at 100% strain following stretching, the film was promptly transferred to a sample holder for solid-state PL measurement. The PL spectrophotometer was utilized in a front face (FF) mode, and the specimen was positioned at a 45° angle in relation to the excitation light. The intensity of red emission was measured at the center of the BGQDs-polymer film, using a fixed excitation wavelength of 550 nm. Subsequently, the film was released from the sample holder to undergo additional mechanical cyclic tests.

Fig. 1
(a) displays a schematic illustrating the application of synthesized BGQDs in the preparation of a self-healing polymer film for LED. The TEM images of GQDs and BGQDs are depicted in Figure S3
(a) and Fig. 1 (b). The results of TEM images revealed that both samples had circular surface morphology and exhibited no apparent aggregation, indicating excellent dispersion. HRTEM analysis showed that clear lattice fringes were present in both samples, and the lattice spacing of GQDs and BGQDs was determined to be 0.21 and 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (002) graphite lattice plane. Additionally, the highly crystalline structure of BGQDs was confirmed by the standard six-fold symmetric pattern revealed by Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis mode (Fig. 1 (b)) [49]. Particle size analysis unveiled the diameter distribution of GQDs and BGQDs, ranging from 2.17 to 5.71 nm and 2.08 to 4.91 nm, respectively. The average particle sizes were determined to be 4.03 nm for GQDs and 3.84 nm for BGQDs.

The thickness and layer number of GQDs and BGQDs were determined through AFM measurements. The regions marked by red lines in Figure S3
(b) and Fig. 1 (c) were selected for the measurements and calculations. The analysis revealed that the thickness of GQDs was approximately 4.1 nm, equivalent to approximately 8–9 layers of graphene. On the other hand, the thickness of BGQDs was approximately 3.3 nm, corresponding to around 6–7 layers of graphene. The introduction of boron doping induced lattice instability, resulting in structural modifications in the material. Consequently, there was an increase in defect density and a reduction in the average thickness of the material [46,47].

Raman spectroscopy was employed to investigate the defect levels of GQDs and BGQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 (d-e), which involved the analysis of Raman mapping and the ID/IG ratio. In Fig. 1 (e), the Raman spectrum of GQDs showed the presence of D and G peaks at 1378 and 1616 cm−1, respectively. On the other hand, the Raman spectrum of BGQDs, the D and G peaks were located at 1381 and 1616 cm−1, respectively, with corresponding ID/IG values of 0.8 and 1.12, determined by the areas under the fitted curves. These findings suggest that the p orbitals of boron atoms interact with the sp2 orbitals of GQDs, resulting in an increased presence of sp3-hybridized carbon bonds and, consequently, an increase in defects. The ID/IG ratio Raman mapping display in Fig. 1 (d) further confirms this, as BGQDs exhibit more green areas (where the color blue is used to indicate a low ID/IG ratio, while the color red is used to represent a high ID/IG ratio. The color green, on the other hand, is used to depict an intermediate ID/IG ratio.) compared to GQDs [50,51].

In order to investigate the elemental composition and chemical bonding of GQDs and BGQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed. The XPS full survey of GQDs and BGQDs in Fig. 2
(a) demonstrated that, in addition to the signals of carbon (C, 287.1 eV), nitrogen (N, 407.1 eV), and oxygen (O, 535.1 eV) in BGQDs, a signal of boron (B, 194.1 eV) was also detected. The corresponding doping concentrations of C, N, O, and B were determined to be 74.38%, 1.61%, 23.51%, and 0.5%, respectively, as shown in Table 1 . This confirmed the successful doping of boric acid as a boron source into GQDs via a hydrothermal method.

To provide a more detailed explanation, all high-resolution XPS spectra were deconvoluted, similar to previous studies by Juang et al [52], and the resulting peak values and area-under-the-curve ratios for GQDs and BGQDs were compiled in Table S1 and S2 . In the XPS spectra of GQDs, the N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks located at 398.9 and 405 eV ( Figure S4
(b)), which corresponded to Graphitic N and NO2, respectively. The C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks located at 284.1, 283.2, and 286.4 eV ( Figure S4
(c)), attributed to C = C, C-N, and C-O, respectively. The O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks located at 532 and 533.8 eV ( Figure S4
(d)), attributed to C = O and C-O/C-NO2, respectively.

In the XPS spectra of BGQDs, the C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into four peaks located at 284.9, 283.1, 286.9, and 289.2 eV (Fig. 2 (b)), corresponding to C = C, C-N, C-O, and C-B, respectively. The N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks located at 399.5, 402.5, and 404.9 eV (Fig. 2 (b)), attributed to Pyrrolic N, Graphitic N, and NO2, respectively. The O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks located at 533 and 535.8 eV (Fig. 2 (c)), attributed to C = O and C-O/B-O/C-NO2, respectively. The B1s spectrum was deconvoluted into the peak of BCO2 located at 193.1 eV (Fig. 2 (d)), which suggests that boron doping is present in the form of BCO2 surrounding the GQDs.

Based on the XPS analysis, it was concluded that boron doping had a significant impact on the surface chemistry of GQDs, leading to the formation of new functional groups and an increase in the number of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups, where the nitrogen originated from the amide functional group in DMF and the signal generated from incomplete detachment of the precursor TNP. The oxygen primarily came from the bonding between the alkyl group introduced by boric acid addition and the precursor TNP. Furthermore, boron doping introduced C-B bonds, which could improve the mechanical and thermal stability of GQDs [53,54].

Fig. 3
(a) and Figure S5
(a) depict the measurement of UV–Vis and PL spectra of GQDs and BGQDs dispersed in PGMEA solution. It was prominently evident from the absorption spectra that boron doping could significantly enhance the absorption capability of visible light within the range of 400 nm to 600 nm. This enhancement could be attributed to the introduction of boron atoms, which induced modifications in the surface structure by altering the non-bonding electrons' n-π* transitions of the adsorbed atoms (C–B, C–O, B–O, C–NO2) [52], thereby resulting in improved absorption. PL spectra showed that GQDs and BGQDs exhibited maximum emission wavelengths of 639 nm and 646 nm, respectively, with excitation wavelengths of 603 nm and 624 nm. These measurements were further elucidated by the emission-excitation 3D maps of the two samples (Fig. 3 (e) and Figure S5
(b)). Moreover, no significant differences were observed between GQDs and BGQDs under daylight. However, under UV light irradiation at 365 nm, GQDs emitted dark yellow light, while BGQDs emitted bright red light. These findings suggest that boron doping didn't noticeably influence the emission wavelengths but resulted in improved luminescence intensity. Interestingly, we also attempted to disperse GQDs and BGQDs in Hexane and Toluene solutions, and similar results were obtained ( Figure S6
(a-b)). To confirm these findings, analysis was performed on GQDs and BGQDs at the same excitation wavelength of 440 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (b) and Figure S6
(c-d), respectively. It was observed that the luminescence intensity was significantly enhanced in all cases. Specifically, when BGQDs were dispersed in PGMEA solution, the luminescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 646 nm was approximately 5.5 times higher than that of GQDs.

Table 2 explained that the change in surface properties resulting from boron doping resulted in an increase in the weight of the intrinsic state lifetime corresponding to the fast decay component from 18% to 53%, while the weight of the defect state lifetime corresponding to the slow decay component decreased from 82% to 47%. This caused a decrease in the weighted average luminescence lifetime after boron doping, which was consistent with the results in Fig. 3 (d). Additionally, fitting the outcomes of Figure S7
(c-d) and performing calculations revealed similar trends in weight variations when GQDs and BGQDs were dispersed in Hexane and Toluene solutions. The obtained results are summarized in Table S3 . Fig. 3 (f) also demonstrates the outstanding storage stability of BGQDs. After one month of storage at room temperature, the PL intensity of BGQDs remained at approximately 90% of the initial luminescence intensity. This indicated that BGQDs were not easily affected by environmental factors, leading to a decline in their luminescence performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mutually synergistic effect of BGQDs/AP to the physical, mechanical, self-healing, and self-recoverability properties of nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Given the richness of oxygen atoms (i.e –OH, C = O, and –COOH groups) and amine atoms (i.e. –NH2, C–NO2, and C–CNC groups), BGQDs does not only serve as red-emitting source, but also as external H-bond cross-linkers that can construct synergistic hydrogen bonding effects with the –OH, –NH, and C = O groups of copolymer molecular chains. Such interactions were capable of boosting thermal stability, resistance under unusual conditions, mechanical, self-healing, and self-recoverability properties, enabling their potential applications in various fields, especially with the role of a film/membrane/matrix [28,45].

The synergistic effect in amphiphilic copolymer matrix before and after incorporating with BGQDs was probed by Attenuated total reflection - Fourier transforms infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra, which further provide strong evidence for the existence of dynamic covalent and non-covalent interactions in nanocomposite system under thermal-triggered self-crosslinking reaction. As shown in Fig. 4
(a), the incorporation of BGQDs into the copolymer system via blending process yielded a significant blue-shift in which the wavenumber of a broad peak attributed to the overlapping –OH and –NH bands decreased from 3419, 3377, to 3373 cm−1 for the pristine copolymer, 0.1% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite, and 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite, respectively, demonstrated the ease of non-covalent dynamic hydrogen bond interactions between copolymer and BGQDs during the simple blending process. Upon thermal-triggered crosslinking reactions for 6 h, these bands were further blue-shifted to 3385, 3363, and 3362 cm−1 for each specimen in the same order, accompanied by the intensity reduction of these peaks. It is noted that the band's intensity of –OH group of the pristine copolymer was obviously decreased after crosslinking reaction, confirming the breakage of the –OH bond and the formation of a covalent bond during the cross-linking reaction ( Figure S8 ). Interestingly, the nanocomposites only showed a slight decrease in the –OH group intensity, implying the abundance of oxygen- and amine-containing groups from BGQDs could compensate for the loss of –OH groups caused by the formation of covalent cross-links. Such a statement is also supported by a significant enhancement in the intensity of free –OH group located at 3737 cm−1 for nanocomposites compared to the pristine copolymer. Furthermore, new bands on nanocomposites detected at 1021–1060, 940, 841, and 739 cm−1 were in agreement with the reported typical vibration bond of B–C, B–OH, B–O–B, and C–B–C, respectively [59,60].

Figure S9 shows the enhancement of 5%-weight-loss temperature (Td5%) and decomposition behaviors on the nanocomposites compared to pristine copolymer as measured via thermogravimetric (TGA) and first derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) analysis. Both 0.1% (w/w)- and 0.5% (w/w)-@BGQDs/AP nanocomposites showed improved thermal stability (Td5%) of 245 and 273 °C compared to a pristine copolymer of 219 °C ( Figure S9
(a)). The enhancement maximum thermal degradation point (Td) due to the additional BGQDs loading in the copolymer system from 381 to 395 °C was further evaluated by DTG analysis on the sharp peak of the second stage ( Figure S9
(b-d)) [28,61]. Subsequently, owing to the higher number of free H-bonds in BGQDs can effectively promote more cross-linking sites, the glass transition temperature (Tg ) values of the nanocomposites were obtained to be higher than that of the copolymer as confirmed using the differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) results. Figure S10 displayed the Tg values of AP, 0.1% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP and 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposites after crosslinking raised from −18.7, −7.9, to −2.1, respectively, implying that the strong covalent bonds generating from cross-linking reaction restrict the movement of the molecular chains.

Profitably by the subzero Tg values and excellent dynamic nature of hydrogen bonds, all specimen films are expected to possess excellent self-healing capabilities at room temperature as the copolymer and nanocomposites chains have adequate mobility to facilitate the reformation of hydrogen bonds at the damaged site. As depicted in Figure S11
(a-c), the scratch recovery process on the AP, 0.1% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP, and 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP thin films coating on the glass surface could be visualized using an optical microscope. Considering the less severe levels of damage to the film matrix surface, short healing periods of 0, 0.5, and 2 h were proposed. It can be observed that the scratches on all sample surfaces became narrow and almost disappeared after 2 h. To examine the synergistic hydrogen bonding effects towards the enhancement of mechanical properties and room-temperature self-healing ability simultaneously, rectangle-shaped films with and without cut by a razor blade into half and pieced back at ambient conditions for 24 h were prepared and subjected to the universal tensile tests. The represented stress–strain curves of the copolymer, 0.1% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP and 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposites films before and after self-healing are shown in Fig. 4 (b). Detailed mechanical parameters and high self-healing efficiency ( ε
SH) values on each tested sample are summarized in Table S4 . The increased covalent bonds at the crosslinking sites between BGQD and copolymer lead to strengthened molecular chain interactions, enhancing the mechanical stress and Young's modulus of the nanocomposites. Lamentably, it also has the effect of limiting the adequate stretching of the chain segments (Fig. 4 (b)). Despite that fact, the visibly reduced strain still reaches above 1900% owing to the densely high chain mobility contributed by the BA segment (Tg < 0 °C, Figure S10 ). Furthermore, 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film with the highest mechanical strength was able to show impeccable self-healing efficiency up to 100% at room temperature. Such phenomenon could be attributed to the reversible reconstruction of physical crosslinks that generate abundant dynamic non-covalent hydrogen bond networks from interactions between the oxygen- and amine-functional groups on BGQD with the NMA segment in the nanocomposite system matrix (as confirmed by FTIR analysis, Fig. 4 (a) and Figure S8 ) and supported by the subzero Tg value.

To further highlight the existence of more physical bonds with increasing BGQDs content in the copolymer system, the self-recoverability was investigated by three times repeated cyclic tensile tests under an extension rate of 50 mm/min with a maximum strain of 100% for each sample ( Figure S12
(a)). Note that the appearance of hysteresis loops can be regarded as the sum of the dissipation energies caused by the breakage of non-covalent interactions (i.e. hydrogen bonds in this case) during the cyclic tests [62,63]. The hysteresis loops decreased as the BGQDs content increased, indicating the enhancement of elastic deformation. These results provide evidence that the addition of BGQDs promotes more covalent and non-covalent interactions in which covalent interactions serve in strengthening and preserving the polymer networks, whereas non-covalent interactions lead to reversible sacrificial H-bonds to dynamically cleave and reform during the loading–unloading tests [45]. Subsequently, the perfectly overlapping triple cycle curves at 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP also confirmed the excellent self-recoverability to restore the mechanical performance to its original state compared with AP and 0.1% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP. Given that our materials could exhibit spontaneous reversible behavior up to 70–80% strain and full recovery with less than a minute of rest time after unloading with an extension rate of 50 mm/min, we further explored the effect of a slower extension rate (10 mm/min) on the stretch-recoverability ( Figure S12
(b)). It was found that decreasing the extension rate during the tensile cyclic test resulted in smaller hysteresis loops. This phenomenon implies lesser non-covalent interaction breakages and subsequently leads to lower dissipated energy. Slowing the extension rate also effectively reduces residual strain and improves immediate self-recovery of up to 80–90% strain as more time is available to reform partially broken hydrogen bonds during the loading–unloading process. These findings prove that our materials possessed good elastic performance. Fig. 4 (c) visually proves the flexibility of the 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite under daily light and 365 nm UV light. The thickness of BGQDs film was determined to be ∼ 300 um by spiral micrometer. In WLEDs packaging, an optimal thin film thickness provides significant advantages. When the film is excessively thick, it absorbs a large amount of light during transmission, leading to a weakening or complete blockage of light energy, thereby hindering the successful generation of visible light. The excellent self-recoverability during the loading–unloading stretching test of over 300% strain under 365 nm UV light was also shown in Video S1 .

To gain an insight into the luminescence intensity changes during stretching, nanocomposite film with low and high content of BGQDs was subjected to PL measurements under 50 times cyclic tests with a maximum strain of 100% and a fixed excitation wavelength of 550 nm (Fig. 4 (d-e)). During loading conditions, the luminescence intensities were reduced by 20% from 1.05 × 106 to 8.55 × 105 for 0.1% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite (Fig. 4 (d)) and reduced by 10% from 1.97 × 106 to 1.72 × 106 for 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite (Fig. 4 (e)). The slight decrease in luminescence intensity during stretching could be attributed to the impact of BGQD deployment over the host matrix shifting towards the stretching direction [48]. Impressively, the intensity values of the recovered nanocomposites thin film were found to be markedly similar to the initial state, suggesting that the excellent elastic properties of the nanocomposites thin film favor high robustness and stability in luminescence properties. In this regard, the amphiphilic copolymer has been successfully proven to be the preferred candidate as a protective polymer matrix to improve the required emission properties and extend the service life of the BGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: x% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film for LED devices
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the unique optical properties and excellent thermal, physical, and mechanical stabilities exhibited by the 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film, a such composite was further applied in WLEDs application. According to the principle of tri-color light, WLEDs were composed of a blue LED chip (450 nm) overlaid with green commercial phosphors and red-emitting nanocomposite film. Fig. 5
(a) illustrated the electroluminescence (EL) spectra obtained at various driving currents, and the corresponding detailed data could be found in Table 3 . All EL spectra exhibited emission peaks centered at 450 nm, 515 nm, and 635 nm, which could be ascribed to the GaN blue chip, (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ phosphors, and 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite, respectively.

With the increase in current from 50 mA to 350 mA, slight changes in the CIE coordinates were observed, which could be attributed to the excitation-independent characteristics of BGQDs [13,23], as depicted in Fig. 5 (b). Furthermore, the variations in CCT and CRI values shown in Fig. 5 (c) were also minimal, confirming the excellent stability of the WLEDs. When the current was set at 300 mA, the WLED exhibited CIE coordinates of (0.2920, 0.3326), which were very close to the ideal coordinates for WLEDs. The correlated color temperature (CCT) was measured at 7739 K, and the color rendering index (CRI) value reached an impressive 89.4. Compared to recent relevant studies in Table 4 , our encapsulated LED devices demonstrated exceptional CRI values, and in Fig. 5 (d), the marked color point closely aligned with the blackbody Planckian locus. These findings collectively demonstrated the competitive performance of our WLEDs based on the synthesized 0.5% (w/w)@BGQDs/AP nanocomposite film in general lighting applications and highlighted their significant potential for practical implementation.

In summary, this study presented the first synthesis of BGQDs using a hydrothermal method with boric acid as the boron source. The BGQDs were incorporated into a polymer matrix to fabricate red-emitting films for application in WLEDs. The BGQDs exhibited superior optical properties compared to GQDs and demonstrated excellent luminescence stability. Despite utilizing a modest strategy to fabricate red-luminescent nanocomposite films, stable luminescent against repetitive mechanical stimuli is achieved favoring long-term usage. In addition, the synergy between the BGQDs functional group and amphiphilic copolymer side chain bestows an enhancement on the thermal stability, mechanical strength and toughness, self-recoverability, and self-healing performance. Based on the aforementioned advantages, satisfactory WLEDs were achieved through a simple fabrication process, which included CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.34), the CCT of 7739 K, and the impressive CRI value of up to 89.4, closely approaching the Planckian locus. These findings have showcased the tremendous potential of BGQDs in the development of the WLEDs field.
